The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company is expected to generate over and above the return that equity investors typically require, then capitalises that stream of “excess” earnings into a value per share.

Book value is $90.74 per share and the stable earnings per share used in the model are $15.97, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 4 analysts. The cost of equity is set at $7.75 per share, so the implied excess return is $8.22 per share. data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider MP Materials (MP) After A 186% One Year Run?
data.description:

Approach 1: MP Materials Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today using a required rate of return, to arrive at an estimate of what the business may be worth right now.

For MP Materials, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $389.08 million. Analyst estimates and subsequent extrapolation point to free cash flow moving to $536.00 million by 2030, with interim projections that remain negative in 2026 and 2027 before turning positive from 2028 onward. Beyond the analyst horizon, Simply Wall St extends these projections using its own assumptions.

Pulling these projected cash flows together and discounting them results in an estimated intrinsic value of about $61.39 per share, compared with the current share price of $66.23. That implies the stock is about 7.9% above the model’s estimate of fair value. This is a relatively small gap in the context of valuation work.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Approach 2: MP Materials Price vs Sales

For profitable companies, price based multiples such as P/S are often used because they link what you pay per share to a fundamental driver like revenue. The P/S ratio in particular can be useful where earnings are volatile or where free cash flow is still developing, since sales are usually more stable than profits.

What counts as a "normal" or "fair" P/S ratio often reflects investors' expectations for future growth and their view of risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher uncertainty tends to line up with a lower figure.

MP Materials currently trades on a P/S ratio of 42.72x, compared with the Metals and Mining industry average of 2.71x and a peer average of 2.15x. Simply Wall St also estimates a Fair Ratio of 6.63x for MP Materials. This Fair Ratio is a proprietary view of what the P/S could be, after considering factors such as earnings growth, industry, profit margins, market cap and company specific risks. Because it incorporates these elements directly, it can often give a more tailored reference point than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry. On this basis, the current P/S of 42.72x sits well above the Fair Ratio of 6.63x.

data.title: Delta Balances Route Cuts And Softer Climate Targets As Costs Bite
data.description:
• Delta Air Lines (NYSE:DAL) is trimming select summer routes as jet fuel prices remain elevated due to ongoing geopolitical conflict.
  • The carrier is also removing a previously stated 2030 target for sustainable aviation fuel from its climate roadmap.
  • Delta is reframing its longer term net zero objective, updating how it presents emissions and decarbonization plans to investors.

Delta Air Lines, one of the largest global network carriers, is adjusting both its schedule and climate roadmap at the same time that the wider airline industry is managing high fuel costs and evolving emissions expectations. Route decisions for the peak summer season can influence aircraft utilization, unit costs, and competitive positioning on key city pairs. At the same time, changes to climate commitments are likely to draw more attention from investors who closely track environmental targets and disclosure quality.

For you as an investor, these moves put a spotlight on how NYSE:DAL balances near term operating efficiency with longer term decarbonization plans. The trimmed route map and updated climate targets may affect perceptions of cost discipline, network strength, and the credibility of the airline's ESG story, which can all feed into how the market views risk and capital allocation over time.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Delta Air Lines by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Delta Air Lines.

3 things going right for Delta Air Lines that this headline doesn't cover.

For Delta, trimming summer routes while jet fuel prices stay high looks like a push to protect unit margins by prioritizing routes that carry stronger yields. The company has already highlighted pressure from fuel costs and reported a Q1 2026 net loss of US$289 million alongside revenue of US$15.9b, so tighter capacity on weaker city pairs can be one way to line up supply with the most profitable demand. At the same time, removing the 2030 sustainable aviation fuel target and reframing net zero as an aspiration introduces questions about how Delta balances cost control with long-term decarbonization, particularly as large corporate customers and lessors increasingly scrutinize emissions plans. Compared with peers such as American Airlines, United Airlines and Southwest Airlines, investors can use these moves to assess whether Delta is leaning more toward near-term earnings protection or toward maintaining leadership in ESG commitments, and how that trade off fits with personal priorities on risk, reputation and access to capital.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Delta Air Lines Narrative

  • The route trims are aligned with the narrative that Delta wants to protect margins and free cash flow by keeping capacity growth flat and matching supply with demand.
  • Pulling back a specific sustainable aviation fuel target could challenge the idea that Delta is investing ahead in long-term operational efficiency and brand differentiation through climate initiatives.
  • The shift in climate targets and messaging around net zero is not explicitly captured in the existing focus on premium, loyalty and international revenue, so its effect on corporate demand or financing conditions may not be fully reflected.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Delta Air Lines to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Prolonged high jet fuel costs combined with route cuts could still leave Delta exposed if demand softens on remaining routes or if fuel prices stay very elevated.
  • ⚠️ Stepping back from a clear sustainable aviation fuel target may weigh on perceptions of Delta’s ESG profile, which could matter for some institutional investors and climate focused customers.
  • 🎁 Focusing capacity on stronger routes can support revenue metrics such as passenger yield and revenue per available seat mile if Delta keeps more seats deployed where pricing is firm.
  • 🎁 Reframing climate goals might give Delta more flexibility to time decarbonization spending alongside its Q2 2026 guidance for 6% to 8% operating margin and US$1.00 to US$1.50 in EPS.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth tracking how route rationalization shows up in unit revenue and margin data in Q2 and beyond, and whether management updates capacity plans again if jet fuel prices stay high. Investors should also watch how large corporate clients and partners respond to the revised climate roadmap, for example through comments on sustainable aviation fuel expectations or travel policies. Comparing Delta’s capacity and ESG moves with those of American Airlines and United Airlines can help you judge whether Delta is taking a more aggressive or conservative stance on both cost control and decarbonization.

  • The stock has shown sharp moves recently, with returns of 9.1% over 7 days, 29.9% over 30 days, 22.8% year to date, 90.4% over 1 year, 18.4% over 3 years, and a 38.4% decline over 5 years.

Recent coverage has focused on how this performance profile might influence expectations around future execution, capital allocation, and the resilience of the business model under different conditions.

Advertisement

Approach 1: QXO Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today’s dollars, aiming to show what the business might be worth based on those projected cash streams.

For QXO, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $183.1 million. Analysts have provided specific Free Cash Flow projections out to 2030, with an estimate for that year of $1,909.8 million, and Simply Wall St then extrapolates further years beyond the explicit analyst horizon using its own growth assumptions.

After discounting these projected cash flows back to today, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $56.53 per share. Compared with a recent share price around $24.22, this implies a 57.2% discount. On this method alone, QXO screens as materially undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests QXO is undervalued by 57.2%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 63 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for QXO.

Approach 2: QXO Price vs Sales

For many profitable companies, the preferred multiple is often based on earnings. However, when earnings are less meaningful or volatile, investors frequently use Price to Sales (P/S) as a cleaner way to compare how the market values each dollar of revenue.

Growth expectations and risk still matter here. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher “normal” or “fair” P/S ratio, while slower growth or higher uncertainty usually points to a lower multiple being more reasonable.

QXO currently trades on a P/S ratio of about 2.56x. That is above both the Trade Distributors industry average of roughly 1.21x and the peer group average of about 2.25x, which at first glance can suggest a richer valuation relative to those simple benchmarks.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for QXO is 5.90x.
